metadata.title: Why One Extra Neutron Makes You 1000x Harder to Superflow

beats.narration_text: Namaste. Liam, in for Bear. Helium four becomes superfluid near two point one seven kelvin. Helium three needs temperatures around a few millikelvin. One neutron changes the scale by roughly a thousand.

props.command: Why does one neutron make helium-3 vastly harder to superflow than helium-4?
props.segment: Why One Extra Neutron Makes You 1000x Harder to Superflow
props.runningText: counting atomic fermions and comparing transition scales…

beats.narration_text: Below the lambda point, helium four can flow through tiny channels and form a creeping Rollin film. Zero viscosity is shorthand: its superfluid component supports dissipationless flow under suitable conditions.
Helium three remains normal near two kelvin. Its superfluid phases appear only around the millikelvin scale, with the exact transition depending on pressure. The temperature gap is the mystery.
Helium four superflows near two kelvin. Helium three needs roughly a few thousandths of a kelvin. They differ by one neutron. Why does the quantum statistics change so much?
Mass is not the decisive clue. Helium three is slightly lighter, but its transition is vastly colder. The crucial difference is whether the entire atom is a boson or a fermion.
Count the fermionic constituents: protons, neutrons, and electrons. An even total gives the composite atom integer spin and bosonic statistics. An odd total gives fermionic statistics.
Helium four has two protons, two neutrons, and two electrons: six fermions. Even. The atom is a boson, so many helium-four atoms may occupy the same complete quantum state.
Helium three has two protons, one neutron, and two electrons: five fermions. Odd. The atom is a fermion, so identical atoms cannot occupy the same complete one-particle state.

Bosonic helium four can develop macroscopic quantum coherence directly. But liquid helium four is strongly interacting, so its superfluid transition is not merely an ideal-gas Bose condensation, and only a fraction occupies the zero-momentum state.
Fermionic helium three cannot use that direct boson route. Pauli exclusion fills distinct states up to a Fermi surface instead of allowing all atoms to crowd into one state.

At much lower temperature, helium-three atoms can form Cooper pairs. Each pair behaves collectively like a boson, enabling a paired superfluid. The fragile pairing scale is why millikelvin cooling is required.

A helium-four cryostat reaches its transition with ordinary pumped-helium techniques. Helium-three superfluidity demands specialized millikelvin refrigeration. One neutron changes not the chemistry, but the quantum statistics and the laboratory.
Your turn. Count the constituents. Helium four has six, so it is bosonic. Helium three has five, so it is fermionic and must pair before it can superflow.
Why One Extra Neutron Makes You 1000x Harder to Superflow.
